title: Jung and Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Jung and Evangelion             Quote Post by Carl Horn » Fri Mar 13, 2009 1:25 am I was searching on the phrase "burn my feelings into film," and I came across the closed Terminal Dogma discussion where someone suggested Jung was relevant to Evangelion, an idea that seemed to be greeted with some dubiousness. I was a little surprised, because that's been my impression from the very beginning, as Jung is something of a bridge between the Kabbalah and psychology: he made reference to it in his works Psychology and Religion , Answer to Job , and especially in his Mysterium Coniunctionis . Now, that doesn't mean that in fact Anno consulted Jung, but it doesn't seem unreasonable that he would know about him either; the rivalry between Jung and Freud is a long-time personal interest of his friend and Eva's co-producer, Hiroyuki Yamaga; this is why in Gunbuster (which Yamaga co-wrote), the character is named Jung-Freud.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Mar 13, 2009 2:11 am Evangelion is Freud, silly. Jung was the complete opposite, sort of. Evangelion ANIMA on the other hand...

[]:             Quote Post by FallenTabris » Fri Mar 13, 2009 3:09 am Jung was Freud's metaphysical foil, in a way. His works to me imply a belief in more spiritual ideas than they state outright. The idea of a great unconscious itself is borderline-spiritual.

[]:             Quote Post by praticehero2010 » Fri Mar 13, 2009 7:27 am It is obvious that Freud's theories play a larger role than Jung's throughout the majority of the series. That isn't to say, however that you can't draw Jungian parallels as well. I don't know if its been discussed before, but the group/hive mind aspect of Instrumentality seems to resemble a physical manifestation of Jung's theory of the collective unconscious.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Fri Mar 13, 2009 8:21 am My sensei told me that Evangelion draws from the work of Nietzsche rather than Jung or Freud.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Fri Mar 13, 2009 11:10 am The difference between Freud and Fraud is one letter. The difference between Jung and Junk is one letter. Please keep these facts in mind.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Fri Mar 13, 2009 11:53 am I don't know if its been discussed before, but the group/hive mind aspect of Instrumentality seems to resemble a physical manifestation of Jung's theory of the collective unconscious.

[]:             Quote Post by praticehero2010 » Fri Mar 13, 2009 1:45 pm @AuraTwilight: I didn't mean to say that they were similar in all respects. I was thinking that in a vaguely Jungian sense, you could call Instrumentality a "Collective Conscoius", where the thoughts and feelings of humanity are shared, rather than the actual Collective Unconscious, which is, like you said, a collection of the fight/flight and the other instincts. I probably should have clarified a bit more. @BobBq: Don't put that as an answer to a psychology question on free association. Been there, done that, and got it wrong.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Mar 13, 2009 2:16 pm BobBQ wrote: Please keep these facts in mind.

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Fri Mar 13, 2009 3:15 pm Methinks the Tree of Life guy will have something to say on this matter.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Mar 13, 2009 3:18 pm Reichu wrote: BobBQ wrote: Please keep these facts in mind.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Fri Mar 13, 2009 5:05 pm praticehero2010 wrote: @AuraTwilight: I didn't mean to say that they were similar in all respects. I was thinking that in a vaguely Jungian sense, you could call Instrumentality a "Collective Conscoius", where the thoughts and feelings of humanity are shared, rather than the actual Collective Unconscious, which is, like you said, a collection of the fight/flight and the other instincts. I probably should have clarified a bit more.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Mar 13, 2009 6:20 pm Jung and Evangelion? No, Jung was the proto-Asuka in Gunbuster.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Fri Mar 13, 2009 6:23 pm Jung Freud , no less.

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Fri Mar 13, 2009 10:53 pm What about the other psychology theories?

[]:             Quote Post by Evangelion217 » Sat Mar 14, 2009 3:06 pm Evangelion is Freud, silly. Jung was the complete opposite, sort of.

[]:             Quote Post by praticehero2010 » Sat Mar 14, 2009 6:26 pm What about the other psychology theories?

[]:             Quote Post by Twsa'teotu » Sat Mar 14, 2009 7:15 pm ...didn't the Japanese creators openly admit that the series actually has nothing to do with Kabbalah or Christian symbology? And that Anno literally wrote the series with a psychology textbook in hand?

[]:             Quote Post by praticehero2010 » Sat Mar 14, 2009 7:39 pm ...didn't the Japanese creators openly admit that the series actually has nothing to do with Kabbalah or Christian symbology? And that Anno literally wrote the series with a psychology textbook in hand?

[]:             Quote Post by Twsa'teotu » Sat Mar 14, 2009 8:38 pm Some? I'm pretty sure they said *all* of it was.

I was a little surprised, because that's been my impression from the very beginning, as Jung is something of a bridge between the Kabbalah and psychology: he made reference to it in his works Psychology and Religion , Answer to Job , and especially in his Mysterium Coniunctionis . Now, that doesn't mean that in fact Anno consulted Jung, but it doesn't seem unreasonable that he would know about him either; the rivalry between Jung and Freud is a long-time personal interest of his friend and Eva's co-producer, Hiroyuki Yamaga; this is why in Gunbuster (which Yamaga co-wrote), the character is named Jung-Freud.
